title: Going Live Checklist
intro: Launch day is here. Just like a theater company checks the lights and sound before opening night, you need to verify everything works before the audience arrives. You're about to share your task manager with the world. But first, let's make sure you haven't forgotten anything critical.

teach.title: The Launch Checklist
teach.body: Before going live, you need to verify: all environment variables are set, HTTPS is enabled, error tracking is configured, there's a way to roll back if something goes wrong, and someone is available to fix issues. Most launch-day disasters come from skipping one of these basics.

teach.title: Going Live Is Like Opening Night
teach.body: A theater company doesn't open the doors without checking the sound system, stage lights, and emergency exits. Going live is your opening night — the audience is real, first impressions matter, and a 10-minute walkthrough beforehand can save you from an embarrassing disaster.

teach.title: Pre-Launch Essentials
teach.code:
[ ] Production env vars set and verified
[ ] HTTPS enabled (automatic on Vercel)
[ ] Error tracking configured (Sentry, etc.)
[ ] Database migrations applied
[ ] Auth working in production environment
[ ] Rollback plan: know how to revert to previous deploy
[ ] Open Graph / social meta tags set
[ ] 404 page configured
teach.annotation: Walk through each item. A 10-minute checklist review can prevent a 10-hour launch-day emergency.

teach.body: Launch day is when your vibecoded project meets the real world. A smooth launch builds trust, attracts early adopters, and sets the stage for your first paying customers.
Launch small, launch ready. Don't announce to thousands if you haven't tested the production environment. Share with 10 people first, fix what breaks, then open the floodgates.

start.narrative: It's 9 AM on launch day. Your task manager is deployed to Vercel and everything looks good. You're about to share the link on social media. But you haven't tested the production environment yet — only development. What do you do?

choices.text: Post the link now — it worked in development, it should be fine
Do a quick smoke test: sign up, create a task, complete it, delete it — all in production
Delay the launch by a week to do extensive QA testing

yolo-recovery.narrative: You posted the link before testing. Within 30 minutes someone tries to sign up and gets a blank screen — the Supabase auth redirect URL is still set to localhost. You take down the post and pull the deploy. What's your move now?

choices.text: Fix the auth redirect URL, redeploy, and run a real smoke test in production before reposting
Fix the redirect URL and repost immediately — no time for another test

smoke-test.narrative: Good call. You try to sign up and the auth flow fails — the OAuth redirect URL is set to localhost. You fix it in the Supabase dashboard, retest, and everything works. How do you decide who gets the link first?

choices.text: Pick 5 friends who will actually try the app and give honest feedback within the hour
Email everyone in your contacts at once to maximize early signups

over-delay.narrative: A week-long delay for a side project kills momentum. By the third day you've already opened five new Figma files for v2 features. You force yourself to compromise: do a focused 2-hour test session today and launch this afternoon.

choices.text: Run the smoke test in production, fix what breaks, and pick a launch group for this evening
Decide one more week of polish won't hurt and push the launch date again

soft-launch-step.narrative: Your soft-launch group is using the app. They surface two small bugs: a typo on the homepage and a confusing button label. While you fix them, what monitoring do you put in place?

choices.text: Wire Sentry to alert on production errors and write down the exact command to roll back the last deploy
Tail Vercel runtime logs in a terminal tab and refresh occasionally
If users complain, they'll tell you on Twitter — no need for tooling yet

social-meta-step.narrative: Bugs are fixed and monitoring is up. You're about to draft the launch tweet. You paste the URL into the compose box and the link preview shows... a generic favicon and no description. What do you fix before the public announcement?

choices.text: Add Open Graph title, description, and image meta tags so the link preview is branded
Skip the meta tags — the URL on its own is enough
Don't fix the meta tags; just attach a screenshot to the tweet manually

announce-step.narrative: Smoke-tested, soft-launched, monitored, branded. How do you do the public announcement?

choices.text: Post on one channel, watch error rates and signups for an hour, then expand to more channels
Post simultaneously to every social channel, newsletter, and forum to maximize reach
Don't actually announce it publicly — let users find it organically

excellent-end.narrative: The launch unfolds calmly: smoke test caught the auth bug, soft-launch friends caught the cosmetic ones, Sentry stays quiet, link previews look polished, and you have a known rollback command ready. By the end of the day, your task manager has its first real users without a single fire to put out.
excellent-end.feedback: Launch day done right. The discipline you applied — verify in production, test with real humans, set up monitoring before the rush, polish the social surface, then announce in waves — is exactly what separates a launch that builds trust from one that burns it.

mediocre-end.narrative: The launch goes okay. The smoke test and soft launch saved you from the worst bugs, but blasting every channel at once means you can't tell what worked, and a bug spike at hour two becomes hard to triage among the noise.
mediocre-end.feedback: Solid foundation, sloppy finish. A staggered announcement gives you signal: which channel sent the most engaged users, where errors spiked, where to double down. Spread it across the day, not the same five-minute window.

weak-end.narrative: You skipped the announcement. Three weeks later, the app has six users — all friends — and you've lost the energy to relaunch. Momentum is the launch's most fragile asset and you let it leak away.
weak-end.feedback: A quiet launch is barely a launch. You did the engineering work to make the app trustworthy; let the world know it exists. Pick one channel, post once, and use the response to plan the next move.
